profile.name: Xian Valencia
profile.bio: I write code by day, log miles by dawn, and spin cranks on weekends. This is where shipping software meets chasing PRs and KOMs — race reports, ride logs, engineering notes, and everything in between.

posts.title: 404 Shoes Not Found: Racing Trilogy Run Leg 2 with a 360 Camera
posts.excerpt: Three days before race day, my Adidas Evo SL got stolen. So naturally, I strapped on a DJI Osmo 360 and vlogged my first half marathon attempt of the year anyway.
body: Some race weeks write themselves. This one wrote a soap opera. Three days before the Santé Barley Trilogy Run Asia Manila Leg 2, my trusted Adidas Evo SL got stolen. Gone. The shoes I'd been saving for a PR attempt, vanished. I didn't lace up again until the starting line itself.

Some context: after running the 16K at Leg 1 two months ago, I fell down the cycling rabbit hole (blame the Giant Revolt 0). Running took a backseat until about two weeks before race day, when I locked back in and trained hard for this 21K. I truly believed a PR was coming. Then the shoe thing happened, and mentally I just wasn't all there anymore.

So I did the only logical thing: I made race day harder by strapping on a DJI Osmo 360 and vlogging a half marathon for the first time. 3:09 AM gun start in Pasay, 26°C feeling like 28°C, 91% humidity — the Manila classic.

The Puma Deviate Nitro 3 stepped up as the understudy. The first 15K actually felt controlled — hovering in the 6:30–7:00 range while juggling a camera. Then kilometers 16 to 18 collected their tax: 7:49, 7:59, and a humbling 8:30. Still had enough for a 5:05/km kick in the final stretch, because ego is a renewable resource.

Final line: 21.19 km in 2:27:34, 6:58/km average, 1,828 calories torched. Missed my PR by 2 minutes — on a week where I lost my shoes, my momentum, and occasionally my breath to a vlog monologue. I'll take it. Maybe that says something too.
Full video below — first attempt at run-vlogging, so expect shaky horizons and heavy breathing. Next stop: Galaxy Manila Marathon Half Marathon, part of the full marathon training block. 🔥

embeds.title: I Tried Vlogging a Half Marathon for the First Time
Santé Barley Trilogy Run Asia Manila Leg 2

posts.title: Galaxy Manila 2026: git push origin/finish-line
posts.excerpt: Galaxy Manila Marathon 2026, half marathon leg — 21.11 km in 2:19:21 at 6:36/km. Flat course, Manila roads, city still half-asleep at gun start.
body: Galaxy Manila Marathon is one of the bigger events on the Metro Manila race calendar — well-organized, well-attended, and a solid benchmark for where your fitness sits mid-year. I lined up for the half marathon leg: 21.11 kilometers of nearly flat city tarmac.

Flat is fast in theory. In practice, 24 meters of total elevation and Metro Manila's road surfaces are a different kind of test — concrete that doesn't give, turns that break your rhythm, and heat that shows up even at a pre-dawn gun start. Crossed the line in 2:19:21, 6:36/km average. A clean finish.

This race came mid-year in a block that's been split between the saddle and the road. Getting back under race conditions was a useful recalibration — a reminder that 21K at threshold pace demands respect, whether you've been logging running miles or cycling kilometers. The legs remembered what to do.
Vlog is on Facebook below, Strava data attached. Filed under: shipped.

posts.title: Unang Reverse Laguna Loop: 175 km and No Ctrl+Z
posts.excerpt: 175.28 kilometers of the Laguna Loop — but backwards. Nine hours in the saddle, 1,186 meters of climbing, and the first time I've ever done it in reverse.
body: The Laguna Loop is a well-worn route among cyclists around Metro Manila — a big circle around Laguna de Bay that most people tackle in a set direction. The 'reverse' variant flips the script: same roads, same distance, entirely different challenge profile. Naturally, I decided to attempt mine for the first time ever, on camera.

175.28 kilometers. 9 hours, 6 minutes, 54 seconds of moving time. That's not a ride, that's a deployment that went sideways and you're stuck watching the build logs scroll by. The average speed held at 19.2 km/h — which sounds respectable until you feel what 170+ km of accumulated fatigue does to your legs in the final stretch.

The elevation was the real story: 1,186 meters of climbing distributed across the entire route. Not brutal by mountain standards, but compound interest on a very long day in the saddle. By the time the bigger climbs arrived, my legs had already spent hours quietly reconsidering their life choices.

The full vlog is below — first time bringing a camera on a ride this long, and probably not the last. If you're curious about the Reverse Laguna Loop, it's worth every kilometer. If you're curious why I keep signing up for things that take nine hours, that's a longer conversation.

posts.title: Sierra Madre Loop: 121 km, 1,915 m, and an Infinite Loop in Sta. Maria
posts.excerpt: 121.51 km through the Sierra Madre range — plus an unplanned detour through Sta. Maria, Laguna. 1,915 meters of climbing and 3,414 calories that didn't ask permission.
body: The Sierra Madre Loop is one of those routes that looks reasonable on paper until the elevation profile renders and you remember that mountains don't care about your training plan. 121.51 km around the Sierra Madre range, with 1,915 meters of climbing baked in — the kind of ride where every smooth descent is just the road gathering its thoughts before the next wall.

The 'x Lost in Sta. Maria, Laguna' in the activity name tells the rest of the story. At some point mid-ride, the route and I had a disagreement. The route won. An unplanned detour through Sta. Maria added distance, added elevation, and added a very specific kind of mental load — the debugging session where you're not sure which road is the right exit and your legs have already spent hours voting to stop.
Final numbers: 6:31:12 of moving time, 18.6 km/h average, 3,414 calories. The elevation made the average speed an honest reflection of the terrain — this wasn't a flat spin, it was a negotiation.
Vlog on Facebook below, Strava data attached.
